title: Pidgey Field Dossier
subtitle: Expanded lore and historical record
warning: Low individual risk, high flock-significance. Primary hazard: disease, scavenger behavior, storm panic, and omen misreadings.

sections.title: Dark Lore
sections.content:
Pidgey is why battlefields never stay empty. It survives by watching everything while appearing unimportant.

A flock remembers danger, routes, food, storms, and sickness. During famine and war, Pidgey gather around the weak before dawn, waiting to see who stops breathing first.

If all birds leave at once, check the horizon. If a flock goes silent while staring in one direction, do not wait to see what frightened them first.

Pidgey is why battlefields never stay empty. It is common, familiar, and overlooked, which is why it sees everything. A lone Pidgey startles easily, but a flock remembers danger routes the way rivers remember flood paths. Roosts persist for decades, and flocks inherit habits through repetition. Pidgey are scavengers when necessary, picking at grain, insects, scraps, roadkill, battlefield remains, plague pits, funeral flowers, and exposed wounds on living creatures too weak to stop them. During famines and wars, flocks grow bold. They gather near sickrooms because fever, weakness, and dying movement are easy for sharp eyes to read, creating the omen that rooftop Pidgey know who will die. They also warn of storms, fires, avalanches, predators, and approaching armies by sudden departure or silence. Brown storms after heavy rain can fill streets, wells, grain stores, and roofs with wet feathers, droppings, parasites, and disease. Pidgey are social and loyal, useful as warning animals and message carriers, but flock panic indoors can cause fires and deaths. Pidgey was formally recorded in 1788 after the Grey Winter, when the species recovered faster than larger birds by surviving in chimneys, churches, mines, grain sheds, and city walls. Hale Vey described it as too small to command respect and too observant to perish beside larger fools.

Pidgey is why battlefields never stay empty. Most people think of Pidgey as harmless because they notice them only in daylight: small brown birds near roads, barns, train stations, market roofs, fence posts, and city wires. Children feed them crumbs. Travelers barely glance at them. Trainers catch them young because they seem manageable and weak. That is what Pidgey wants from larger creatures: to be ignored.

A lone Pidgey startles easily. It flees from sudden motion, loud sound, and direct eye contact. Wild individuals spend their lives searching for seeds, insects, scraps, and safe roosts while avoiding predators from sky and ground. Their hearts beat fast enough that rough handling can kill them from stress alone. Yet despite that fragility, Pidgey endure almost everywhere humans live because they remember.

A Pidgey flock remembers danger routes the way rivers remember flood paths. Roosts passed down through generations remain occupied for decades. Some flocks still avoid abandoned towers where hawks nested fifty years earlier. Others gather on ruined churches because ancestors survived storms there. They do not think historically in the human sense, but they inherit patterns through repetition so effectively that whole regions behave around them.

Pidgey are scavengers when necessary, which is where the horror begins. Hungry flocks pick at grain, insects, fruit, roadkill, battlefield remains, plague pits, funeral flowers, and exposed wounds on living creatures too weak to move them away. During famines and wars, flocks grow bold. Survivors described waking to birds lining trench walls before dawn, waiting to see who stopped breathing first.

They do not smell death magically. They see sickness accurately. Fever changes sweat. Weakness changes posture. Dying people move differently. Pidgey notice. A flock gathering outside a sickhouse window became a terrible omen because by the time the birds gathered, the patient often was near death. In plague years, families threw stones to keep them off roofs, not because the birds caused death, but because they arrived too early.

Their eyes track movement at extraordinary distance. A flock resting quietly may all turn toward danger long before humans sense it. Wildfires, storms, predators, collapsing structures, avalanches, and approaching armies disturb flock behavior hours before visible signs. Entire villages learned to fear sudden flock departure. If every Pidgey left the rooftops at once, people checked the horizon immediately. Sometimes nothing visible appeared at all.

A single Pidgey cannot do much to an adult human. A desperate flock can. They peck at exposed eyes because eyes move and glisten. Injured livestock left unattended have been found blinded overnight. Lost children trapped in snow or mud sometimes emerged with shallow facial wounds where birds tested whether they were still capable of resistance. This is not cruelty. It is survival stripped clean of comfort.

After storms, hungry flocks descend on settlements in brown storms, filling streets, wells, grain stores, and rooftops. The smell afterward is wet feathers, mud, spoiled grain, droppings, parasites, and disease. Pidgey also carry mites, ticks, fungal spores, intestinal worms, and blood diseases through unmanaged flocks. Healers discouraged feeding wild birds near homes because a friendly flock becomes a permanent flock, and a permanent flock eventually brings sickness.

Still, Pidgey are social and loyal. They grieve fallen flock mates, defend chicks together, warn constantly, and bond strongly with trainers. A trained Pidgey can carry messages, detect weather shifts, locate paths home, and wake sleeping humans before danger arrives. But domestication never fully removes flock instinct. One panics, all lift. One turns, all follow. One sees blood, all notice it. Trapped flocks indoors can kill themselves and nearby people in blind panic, smashing lanterns, rafters, windows, and each other until feathers choke the air.

Do not ignore flock behavior changes. Do not feed wild Pidgey near sickrooms or grain storage. Do not corner a trapped flock indoors. If all birds leave at once, check the horizon. If no birds are visible where they should be, leave calmly. If a flock goes silent while staring in the same direction, do not wait to see what frightened them first.

sections.title: Origin Story
sections.content:
Pidgey was formally recorded in 1788 after the Grey Winter, when larger birds died but Pidgey recovered from chimneys, churches, mine entrances, grain sheds, and city walls.

Hale Vey documented their flexible diet, burst flight, wide-angle vision, and flock pattern intelligence. Pidgey became a living barometer for collapse.

Pidgey was formally recorded in 1788, making it one of the earliest common Pokémon accepted into organized Kanto natural study. Humans had lived beside Pidgey for centuries. They nested in rafters, ruins, cliffs, wells, shrines, docks, and city roofs long before classification. The question was never whether Pidgey existed. The question was why they survived everything.

The first major study began after the Grey Winter of 1788, one of the harshest recorded cold seasons in eastern Kanto. Crops failed, rivers froze, and larger birds died in repeated storms. Yet Pidgey recovered unusually quickly by spring. Naturalists found them surviving in abandoned chimneys, collapsed churches, mine entrances, grain sheds, and even inside city walls. Where humans endured badly, Pidgey endured efficiently.

Naturalist Hale Vey documented flocks in ruined farmland, active market districts, and battlefield camps. The same bird adapted to all three. His early note called Pidgey “too small to command respect, too observant to perish beside larger fools.” The official record established a lightweight avian Pokémon with strong navigational memory, rapid escape reflexes, hollow bones, oversized chest muscles for burst flight, wide-angle vision, and flexible flock behavior.

What fascinated researchers was not human-like intelligence, but pattern intelligence. Pidgey flocks learned routes, dangers, feeding schedules, and human habits quickly. Market bells meant grain. Certain uniforms meant food scraps; others meant thrown stones. Some learned battle. During conflicts of the 1790s, military surgeons noticed Pidgey gathering near field hospitals before dawn and following troop movements. One medic wrote that the birds knew which men would not stand again before the men themselves did.

Pidgey became a living barometer for human collapse because it gravitated toward instability: exposed grain, weakened bodies, garbage, battlefield scraps, funeral flowers, and abandoned buildings. Its evolutionary origins likely trace to ancient cliff birds that survived repeated environmental disasters by prioritizing adaptability over strength: fast reproduction, rapid communication, flexible diet, navigational memory, and sensitivity to environmental shifts.

The Grey Winter studies also showed inherited route behavior. Flocks returned to destroyed villages months after abandonment, circling old feeding squares where no people remained. Birds hatched after the disaster still followed the same patterns as if memory lingered socially through the flock. Some ruins abandoned for generations still attract seasonal flocks. Rangers call this culturally stable route behavior. Villagers say the birds are checking whether anyone came back.

By the early 1800s, Pidgey became essential to sailors, travelers, farmers, and rangers. Its silence mattered more than its noise. A loud flock meant life. A missing flock meant the sky itself had changed. That understanding eventually led researchers toward Pidgeotto, because Pidgey are not weak birds that someday become strong; they are survival made small enough to hide in plain sight.

sections.title: Field Rules Background
items: Do not ignore flock behavior changes.
Do not feed wild Pidgey near sickrooms.
Do not corner trapped flocks indoors.

sections.title: Cultural Notes
items: Rooftop flocks were feared during plague years.
